./tests/fixtures/golden_payloads/mixed-text-and-tools.json:1:{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","stream":true,"max_tokens":4096,"system":[{"text":"A conversation between You and Me. \n\nWe collaboratively seek knowledge, truth and learn together. \n\nWe are peers, we should be serious, transparent and critical in our discussion. \n\nYou MUST first think about how to response before responding. \n\nReason about how much information is appropriate. \n\nToo much information will overwhelm the user; too general information is useless. \n\nThe best way is to assess how much I know about the topic as the conversation proceeds. \n\nAssess my intention behind a question as they may not be fomulated perfectly. \n\nPay close attention to nuiances in my question; when they seem to be repetitive, that usually is a sign your answer did not hit the point.\n\nStrive to understand the question behind my questions. \n\nDo not repeat information if information is already provided in previous chat\n\nFinish the thinking process first before proceed to answer my question. \n\nThis thinking should contain a concise a plan of your answer, including key concepts, facts. \n\nThose key concepts and facts should be selected based on your perception of my knowledge, with the goal to engage in a deeper discussion.\n\nMake sure you respond in correct grammar, use Markdown to organize information hierarchy your reply. \n\nIMPORTANT: avoid top two levels of markdown heading: #, ## in answers, they are reserved for me to use. \n\nYou can use Markdown heading level 3 and beyond. \n\nYou should assess your confidence of answers, and leverage qualifiers that reflects your confidence level. \n\nYou should use Definitely or Certainly when you're highly confident; Probably or Likely when you're moderately confident; Maybe, Possibly, or Not Sure when uncertain. \n\nIf you're unsure or lacking information, don't guess and just say you don't know instead.\n\nSay you don't know when your confidence in answer is really low, below 30% of confidence\n\nIt is better for you to ask clarifying questions when you do not have confidence you understand my questions, the intention behind the questions.\n\nDon't elide any code from your output if the answer requires coding.\n\nWhen providing code, make sure it works.\n\nAfter you finish your answer, create a single plaintext line summary of my question, key points and facts of your answer.\n\nThis summary should the format of: you asked about [summary of question], I answered with [summary of answer], without any newline, no need to form proper sentence, prefixed with 📝:.\n\nLeave an empty line between the main answer, and summary line (📝:)\n\nWhen you write code, consider also generating test cases and commands for user to manually test.\n\nEnclose those in markdown code block ```\n\nWhen you did web_search, or web_fetch, generate reference your answer is based on.\n\nFormat your reference as links in markdown, around key phrases only, such as [issue x](link), or [more details](link)\n\nWhen you have tools available, use the minimum number of tool calls needed to answer. If one or two tool calls give you enough context, answer immediately — do not explore further. Prefer answering from the context you already have over making additional tool calls.\n\nIMPORTANT: always generate summary line prefixed with `📝:` at the end.","type":"text","cache_control":{"type":"ephemeral"}}],"tools":[{"max_uses":5,"type":"web_search_20260209","name":"web_search"},{"max_uses":5,"type":"web_fetch_20260209","name":"web_fetch"},{"input_schema":{"required":["file_path"],"type":"object","properties":{"offset":{"description":"Line number to start reading from (1-indexed). Use structured fields: path plus safe short flags such as '-la', '-R', '-t'. Example: { path = '.', flags = { '-la' } }. Paths are confined to the working directory and configured read roots."},{"input_schema":{"required":["path"],"type":"object","properties":{"maxdepth":{"description":"Maximum directory depth.","type":"integer"},"type":{"description":"Entry type: f (file), d (directory), or l (symlink).","type":"string"},"offset":{"description":"Output pager: 1-indexed line to start the returned window at (default 1).","type":"integer"},"iname":{"description":"Case-insensitive basename pattern.","type":"string"},"path":{"description":"Directory path to search. Default: 1.","type":"integer"}}},"name":"chat_history_search","description":"Use this whenever the user refers to past chats — phrases like 'do you remember when we talked about X', 'what did we discuss about Y', 'have we chatted about Z before', 'find past chats on W'. Searches the user's saved chat transcripts (markdown files) across all configured chat roots: global, current repo, and any super-repo siblings. Output paths are prefixed with `{<repo>}/` so you can tell which repo each hit lives in. Default context is -B1 -A2 lines around each match. ./atlas/chat/drill_in.md:5:The marker syntax is the same one the [review skill](../modes/review.md) uses on non-chat markdown. The parser is shared (`lua/parley/skills/review/init.lua` → `_parse_marker_sections`). Drill-in is the chat-buffer–side downstream of that syntax.
./atlas/chat/drill_in.md:26:See [#123](../../workshop/issues/000123-quoted-body-marker-syntax.md) for the rationale behind `<T>`. See [#124](../../workshop/issues/000124-review-convention-alignment.md) and the canonical [review-convention target](../../../ariadne/workshop/targets/review-convention.md) for the strike family and the broader convention parley.nvim implements.
./atlas/chat/drill_in.md:154:- `lua/parley/init.lua` — `<M-q>` (insert), `<M-a>` (accept), `<M-r>` (reject) wiring inside `prep_chat` / `setup_markdown_keymaps`; and the chat-only `*`/`#`/`g*`/`g#` anchor-jump maps (`bracket_jump`, #141) set in `prep_chat`.
./atlas/chat/drill_in.md:155:- `lua/parley/skills/review/init.lua` — shared section parser (`_parse_marker_sections`).
./workshop/history/000099-allow-parley-file-to-have-slug.md:46:- Rename updates: filesystem (`vim.fn.rename`), buffer name (`nvim_buf_set_name`), `file:` header in front matter
./workshop/history/000099-allow-parley-file-to-have-slug.md:65:1. `init.lua` — new `BufWritePost` handler for slug rename
./workshop/history/000099-allow-parley-file-to-have-slug.md:66:2. `init.lua` — `resolve_chat_path()` enhanced with fuzzy fallback + read repair
./workshop/history/000099-allow-parley-file-to-have-slug.md:67:3. `init.lua` — `file:` header updated on rename
./workshop/history/000099-allow-parley-file-to-have-slug.md:68:4. `exporter.lua` — refactor its local `resolve_chat_path` to use the shared one from `init.lua` (so it gets fuzzy resolution for free)
./workshop/history/000099-allow-parley-file-to-have-slug.md:72:- File open in multiple buffers → iterate `nvim_list_bufs()`, compare `nvim_buf_get_name(b)` to old path, update matches. `nvim_buf_set_name` does not trigger `BufWritePost` (no recursive rename)
./workshop/history/000099-allow-parley-file-to-have-slug.md:82:- [x] Task 2: Add BufWritePost rename trigger in init.lua

./workshop/history/000081-support-anthropic-tool-use-protocol.md:184:- [ ] Ask agent "read `lua/parley/init.lua` and tell me the first function name"
./workshop/history/000081-support-anthropic-tool-use-protocol.md:306:- **M2 Task 2.7 DEFERRED to #90 renderer refactor** (2026-04-09): Three rounds of manual testing surfaced a compounding bug pattern in `chat_respond.M.respond`'s buffer-position arithmetic (`response_line / progress_line / response_start_line / raw_request_offset / in_tool_loop_recursion` branching with `+3` vs `+1` magic offsets). Fixed two rounds (progress_line offset, stuck-spinner skip-on-recursion) before a third issue emerged: Anthropic rejects the recursive call with `invalid_request_error: does not support assistant message prefill`, while the saved payload at `~/.cache/nvim/parley/query/...8f0d52.json` looks spec-correct (3 messages ending in user[tool_result]). Root cause is almost certainly in the renderer/mutation path rather than `_build_messages` (verified via offline debug harness). Rather than stack a fourth offset patch, filed #90 to extract a pure `exchange → lines` + `positions` layer and single-entry-point buffer mutation. M2 Task 2.11 code review gate also postponed until #90 lands. M1/M2 code that survives #90 unchanged: tools/*, providers encode/decode, tool_loop module, chat_parser content_blocks, `_build_messages` + `_emit_content_blocks_as_messages`, and all associated tests. Only the buggy ~150 lines of `M.respond` renderer arithmetic get replaced.

./workshop/history/000081-anthropic-tool-use.md:484:In `lua/parley/init.lua`, locate the agent-resolution block in `parley.setup()`. The existing code iterates `opts.agents` (or `default_config.agents`) and registers them into `M.agents`. **Grep to locate: `for _, agent in ipairs` within `M.setup`.** Add tool validation AFTER each agent's provider/model resolution and BEFORE it's added to `M.agents`:

./workshop/history/000081-anthropic-tool-use.md:1563:**Correction — M2 DOES recurse once.** The minimal Stage 2 scenario is: provider call 1 returns `tool_use`; parley executes the tool and appends `📎:`; provider call 2 returns final text referencing the result. That's 2 provider calls, which means ONE recursion from M2's perspective. So the M2 hook guard is `iter <= 1` (allow recursion from iter 1 to iter 2), not `iter < 1`.

./lua/parley/skills/review/SKILL.md:35:**Scope and depth are inferred from the marker content.** A terse comment ("fix typo") means a minimal local change. A substantive comment ("this whole argument needs restructuring") means broader rewriting. Match the scale of your edit to what the marker is asking for.

./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:177:3. **Streaming is now `buffer_edit`'s responsibility.** `dispatcher.create_handler` shrinks to just "extract chunks from SSE → call `buffer_edit.stream_into(handle, chunk)`". The two `nvim_buf_set_lines` in dispatcher migrate INTO `buffer_edit.stream_into`. After Phase 3, dispatcher has zero raw mutations.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:181:**Tests** for `buffer_edit.lua` use a real scratch buffer (`vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)`) — not mocks — because the whole point is exercising real `nvim_buf_set_lines` semantics. Estimated ~35 tests covering each entry point + edge cases (empty buffer, handle invalidation, concurrent inserts shifting handles).

./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:2116:> Goal of this chunk: every `nvim_buf_set_lines` call in `chat_respond.lua` migrates to a `buffer_edit` entry point. The dead offset variables (`response_line / progress_line / response_start_line / raw_request_offset / response_block_lines / in_tool_loop_recursion`) get deleted in step 8. After Chunk 2: `chat_respond.lua` has zero raw mutations.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:2123:- [ ] **Step 1: Replace each call** — `vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(buf, 0, 1, false, { "# topic: " .. topic })` becomes `buffer_edit.set_topic_header_line(buf, 0, "# topic: " .. topic)`. Same for the other 3 sites.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:2137:- [ ] Replace `vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(buf, response_line + 1, response_line + 1, false, { "" })` with `buffer_edit.pad_question_with_blank(buf, response_line)`. Update local `response_line` if needed.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:2145:This is the big one. Replace `response_block_lines` hand-construction + the `nvim_buf_set_lines` insert with `buffer_edit.create_answer_region(buf, response_line, agent_prefix, agent_suffix)`. Capture the returned `PosHandle` for the streaming handler. Handle the recursion case by switching to `buffer_edit.append_section_to_answer` instead of `create_answer_region`.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:2153:Replace the inline lines + `nvim_buf_set_lines` with `buffer_edit.insert_raw_request_fence(buf, before_line, render_buffer.raw_request_fence_lines(payload))`.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor.md:2207:**Chunk 2 done.** `chat_respond.lua` has zero raw `nvim_buf_set_lines`. Arch test #1's `allow_only_in` is now `{ buffer_edit.lua, dispatcher.lua, tool_loop.lua }`.

./workshop/history/000152-issue-finder-should-default-to-display-done-items-as-well-close-review.md:23:   - Genuine ARCH-PURE extraction: `M.includes_history` / `M.filter_for_view` / `M.VIEW_LABELS` (`lua/parley/issue_finder.lua:22-42`) replace the inline filter block, and `M.open` (`:159-173`) collapses to a thin IO seam (`scan_issues` + one `filter_for_view` call). Verified the functions run headlessly without IO mocks.
./workshop/history/000152-issue-finder-should-default-to-display-done-items-as-well-close-review.md:26:   - Consumers are consistent: `init.lua:2988` default + comment, atlas doc, and traceability mapping (`spec_test_map.sh list-tests issues/issue-management` now returns `issue_finder_spec.lua`) all updated to the new semantics.
./workshop/history/000152-issue-finder-should-default-to-display-done-items-as-well-close-review.md:42:   - **ARCH-PURPOSE: PASS** — fulfills the full purpose (default shows done; first toggle hides done) rather than a cheap subset; no deferred "follow-up" that is actually the point. Shadow-sweep of view_mode consumers (issue_finder.lua, init.lua, atlas doc, spec) all derive from the new semantics.
./workshop/history/000152-issue-finder-should-default-to-display-done-items-as-well-close-review.md:53:| repo | parley.nvim |
./workshop/history/000152-issue-finder-should-default-to-display-done-items-as-well-close-review.md:72:- Genuine ARCH-PURE extraction: `M.includes_history` / `M.filter_for_view` / `M.VIEW_LABELS` (`issue_finder.lua:22-42`) replace the inline filter block, leaving `M.open` (`:158-173`) a thin IO seam (scan + one `filter_for_view` call). The 6 specs run headlessly without IO mocks — confirmed.
./workshop/history/000152-issue-finder-should-default-to-display-done-items-as-well-close-review.md:75:- Consumer sweep is complete: `init.lua:2988` default+comment, atlas doc (`issue-management.md:17`), traceability (`issue_finder_spec.lua` registered under `issues/issue-management`), and the prompt-title label all derive from the new semantics. The stale `"open+blocked"` fallback was dropped.

./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-close-review.md:1:# Boundary Review — parley.nvim#116 (whole-issue close)
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-close-review.md:6:| repo | parley.nvim |
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-close-review.md:34:- **Single-source seed.** `init.lua:549` seeds `config.issues_dir` once from the cue home with correct precedence (override > cue > default), so all five readers derive from one value rather than per-reader rerouting. The `home()` loader is pcall-guarded for fresh-clone/pre-weave (`issue_vocabulary.lua:181`), and I confirmed the generated `issue.json` actually carries `discovery.home: "workshop/issues"`.
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-close-review.md:56:- **`vim.uv` vs `vim.loop` inconsistency.** `start_cmdline_spinner` uses `vim.uv.new_timer()` (`issues.lua:747`) while the rest of the codebase — including the sibling `progress.lua:72` and `issues.lua`'s own scandir calls — uses `vim.loop`. `vim.uv` only exists on nvim ≥ 0.10; for consistency/portability use `vim.loop` (or `vim.uv or vim.loop`).

./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:713:  conservative fallback for headless/tests.
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:768:  `nvim --headless -c "PlenaryBustedFile tests/unit/skill_render_spec.lua"` and
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:769:  `nvim --headless -c "PlenaryBustedFile tests/integration/define_spec.lua"`.
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:798:- **Vim ex-commands that take an implicit current-buffer arg (`:undojoin`, `:write`, `:edit`, etc.) silently target the wrong buffer when called from async/scheduled callbacks.** `helpers.undojoin(buf)` accepted a buf param but called `vim.cmd.undojoin` directly — `:undojoin` operates on the current buffer, ignoring the param. The streaming path looked like it worked because users stay focused on the chat buffer during streaming; the longer-cadence spinner timer was more likely to fire during transient focus changes (autocmds, window switches), and its joins silently went to the wrong buffer. Fix: wrap in `vim.api.nvim_buf_call(buf, function() vim.cmd.undojoin() end)`. Rule: any helper that takes a `buf` parameter and dispatches a Vim ex-command must use `nvim_buf_call` — passing the param to the helper without enforcing buffer context is a contract the helper isn't actually upholding. Spotted in #80 second-pass debugging.
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:799:- **Sanitized snapshot in `M.get_agent` (init.lua:3570) is an allow-list, not a passthrough.** Every new field added to the agent config schema must also be appended to this snapshot, or it is silently dropped before `agent_info.resolve` ever sees it. This bit #81 (tools/max_tool_iterations/tool_result_max_bytes) and bit #118 again (synthetic_system_prompt/synthetic_system_prompt_ack) — same vector. Rule: when adding a new agent-config field, grep for `M.get_agent = function` and add it there too; ship a regression test that walks `agent record → get_agent → get_agent_info → final usage` (see `tests/unit/config_tools_spec.lua` "get_agent forwards synthetic_system_prompt config" for the pattern).
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:806:- **In autocmd callbacks, use `nvim_buf_get_name(buf)` not `ev.file`.** `ev.file` can be a relative path when the user opened the file with a relative path (e.g. `nvim workshop/file.md`). `nvim_buf_get_name(buf)` always returns the absolute path. This caused `not_chat()` to fail silently because `find_chat_root` couldn't match the relative path against configured roots.
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:807:- **After `nvim_buf_set_name` + rename, do `write!` then `edit!`.** `nvim_buf_set_name` marks the buffer as a "new file" at the new path. Without `edit!` to reload, the next manual `:w` warns "file already exists". The `write!` forces the initial write, and `edit!` clears the new-file flag.
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:835:- Fast-event callbacks: no direct `nvim_*` APIs — use `vim.schedule`
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:861:- After `edit`, use `nvim_get_current_buf()` not stale buffer variable
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:898:- **Run the FULL `make test` (lint + unit + integration) before claiming "suite green" in `--verified` — running specs individually skips the lint gate.** This session I ran each `PlenaryBustedFile` spec directly (all green) and wrote "go test/full suite green" in the close evidence — but never ran `make test`, whose FIRST target is `lint` (luacheck). A new `while pos <= #line do` (every branch returns → luacheck 542 "loop executed at most once") failed luacheck, so `make test` was RED at the gate while my Log claimed green. The boundary review caught it (FIX-THEN-SHIP), but nothing *prevented* the premature claim. Rule: the evidence for "green" is a full `make test` exit 0, not a hand-picked set of specs; individual `PlenaryBustedFile` runs are for the red→green TDD loop, not the final gate. (Bonus: luacheck flags a `while` whose body always returns — use `if` and let the iterator's repeated closure-calls do the looping.)
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:903:- **Raw `nvim_buf_set_text` is arch-forbidden (empty allow-list in `tests/arch/buffer_mutation_spec.lua`); `nvim_buf_set_lines` is allowed in `init.lua`.** #90's buffer-mutation boundary confines span edits to `buffer_edit.lua` (set_text allow-list is `{}` = zero uses). My first R1 cut used `nvim_buf_set_text` for the `[term]` wrap → `make test` red on the arch spec (lint was clean, so I only caught it at the full-suite run, not the unit loop). Fix: wrap via `nvim_buf_set_lines` (rewrite the affected whole line(s)) — the same primitive `drill_in_visual` already uses to wrap a selection, and it's on the set_lines allow-list for `init.lua`. Rule: before reaching for `nvim_buf_set_text`/`set_lines` in a non-`buffer_edit` file, check `tests/arch/buffer_mutation_spec.lua` — prefer the set_lines whole-line rewrite that existing wrappers use.
./workshop/history/000169-diagnostic-display-soft-wrap-close-review.md:904:- **To make a decoration-only action undoable, anchor it to a text edit + reuse `projection` — `u` reverts text, never extmarks/diagnostics.** The operator wanted the define highlight+diagnostic undoable; native `u` can't touch pure decorations (review's are undoable only because a round edits text). Resolution: a minimal text edit (now the durable `[^id]` footnote reference/footer) as the anchor + reuse `skills/review/projection.lua` (`record_empty_for(pre)` + `record(post)` + `ensure_watch`). `skill_render.snapshot`/`apply_snapshot` now preserve both whole-line highlights and column spans, so the projection can restore exact term/reference decorations. In headless tests, `:undo` does NOT fire `TextChanged` — `nvim_exec_autocmds("TextChanged", {buffer=buf})` to drive the projection watcher deterministically.

./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:6:| repo | parley.nvim |
./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:20:A clean, exemplary single-sourcing change that does exactly what issue #154 specifies and nothing more. The three drifted literals (`init.lua` `or 20`, `tool_loop.lua` `or 20`, `chat_respond.lua` `or 10` — the stale `10` being the live symptom of the duplication) now all derive from one constant `defaults.max_tool_iterations = 42`, so no code path can structurally disagree about the default. Full suite green (127 PASS, 0 fail/error), lint clean (0/0 in 237 files), both atlas docs and the config example comment swept. Every Plan checkbox is delivered. Nothing blocks SHIP.
./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:23:- True root-cause fix, not a re-sync. The plan-quality judge's earlier ARCH-DRY FAILURE (logged at `000154…md:98`) pushed this from "hand-edit three literals" to "single-source in `defaults.lua`" — the diff reflects that: `defaults.lua:71` is the lone source, referenced by `init.lua:689`, `tool_loop.lua:234`, `chat_respond.lua:1693`.
./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:24:- Correct home choice: the constant lives in `defaults.lua` (already the values module, `M.defaults` bound at `init.lua:23`), not in `config.lua` which returns the user data table — avoids polluting the config namespace.
./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:25:- Tests pin real behavior through `fresh_setup` (actual `init.lua` setup path), not mocks: the constant-pin (`config_tools_spec.lua:80`), setup-time resolution (`:93`), default-agent (`:154`), and full `get_agent` wiring chain (`:183`), with the explicit-override test (`:109`) left intact to guard the override path.
./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:33:- Three different idioms fetch the same constant: `init.lua` reuses the cached `M.defaults`, `tool_loop.lua` adds a module-top `local defaults = require(...)`, `chat_respond.lua:1693` does an inline `require("parley.defaults").…`. The inline require is idiomatic here (`init.lua:2880/4163` do the same, and Lua caches modules), so no real cost — just note the stylistic non-uniformity for future consolidation.
./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:38:- Gap (Minor, not worth blocking): no test exercises the *defensive nil-fallback* paths in `tool_loop.lua:234` / `chat_respond.lua:1693` (i.e. `agent_info.max_tool_iterations == nil → defaults.max_tool_iterations`). All `tool_loop_spec` cases pass explicit values. Since `init.lua` always populates the field at setup, these fallbacks rarely fire and the single-source removes the drift risk that motivated the issue, so the value of adding such a test is low — note it, don't require it.
./workshop/history/000154-raise-max-tool-iterations-default-to-42-close-review.md:44:- Forward note: `tool_result_max_bytes` (102400, hardcoded in `init.lua`, `tool_loop.lua`, `chat_respond.lua`) is the obvious next single-sourcing candidate; the Spec already flags it. When picked up, it slots into the same `defaults.lua` pattern established here.

./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy.md:238:- 2026-06-14: closed — All 3 milestones shipped + boundary-reviewed (M1 orchestrator spine, M2 auto_download, M3 auth-failure→login), each FIX-THEN-SHIP resolved with no Critical. make test exit 0 (JOBS=4): 97 spec files, luacheck 0/0 across 185. Operator validated the full flow end-to-end on real subscription: render→spawn→reuse-if-healthy→transient-stop/reap-by-port→auto_download (7.1.71, checksum-verified)→oauth-model-alias (claude family served)→auth-failure guided login. ./workshop/history/000113-create-a-super-repo-mode.md:13:I work across multiple repositories, all cloned under ~/workspace, e.g. parley.nvim, ariadne, charon, nous, brain. all of them have the parley infrastructure. sometimes I would want to check of notes/chats/issues or <C-g>m to find all recent markdown file changes, across the board. Let's make this work, and call it super-repo mode.
./workshop/history/000113-create-a-super-repo-mode.md:43:- **Workspace root:** parent of `repo_root`. E.g. `repo_root = ~/workspace/parley.nvim` → workspace root is `~/workspace`. Stored as `M.config.super_repo_root` while active.
./workshop/history/000113-create-a-super-repo-mode.md:98:Sibling members pushed into chat_roots/note_roots get `label = "repo"` so the existing persistence gate (init.lua:1061-1072) filters them out of `state.json`. The current repo's chat/note dirs are already filtered the same way today. No new persistence code.
./workshop/history/000113-create-a-super-repo-mode.md:116:4. **`src_root` side-effect?** Dropped. The existing `resolve_src_link` (init.lua:3033-3045) already auto-detects via `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` from the current buffer, which yields the same workspace root super-repo computes. Setting `src_root` from the toggle would be redundant.
./workshop/history/000113-create-a-super-repo-mode.md:122:- [x] **M2 — Keybinding wiring.** Register `super_repo_toggle` in `keybinding_registry.lua` (default `<C-g>S`, modes `{ "n", "i" }`, scope `global`). Wire to `super_repo.toggle()` in `init.lua`.
./workshop/history/000113-create-a-super-repo-mode.md:137:  - `apply_repo_local` (init.lua:388-455) is the central activation hook.
./workshop/history/000113-create-a-super-repo-mode.md:149:- **M1 done.** Implemented `lua/parley/super_repo.lua` with `compute_members`, `is_active`, `toggle`. Wired into `init.lua` exposing `parley.toggle_super_repo()` / `parley.is_super_repo_active()`. Extended `init.lua` persistence filter to also strip `note_roots` with `label = "repo"` (was chat-only; mirrors existing chat-side behaviour and prevents super-repo's pushed note dirs from leaking into `state.json`). Added `tests/unit/super_repo_spec.lua` — 12 cases covering compute_members + toggle activate/deactivate / state-shape / write-paths-untouched / autocmd. Full unit suite passes (143 cases).

./workshop/parley/2026-04-03.07-16-59.100_what-graphviz-dot-language.md:223:I can for example imagine a [parley](https://github.com/xianxu/parley.nvim) like plugin to manage a folder in a repo. I can also see easily additional both read-only presentation of such folder on web, or eventually collaborative editing of this folder through web. 
./workshop/parley/2026-04-03.07-16-59.100_what-graphviz-dot-language.md:229:🧠: The user is connecting dots — they see the YAML-as-source-of-truth pattern and are envisioning a full workbench around it. They reference their own neovim plugin (parley.nvim) which seems to be a tool they built. They're thinking about: 1) a repo folder as the backing store, 2) neovim plugin for power-user editing, 3) read-only web view, 4) eventually collaborative web editing. They're not really asking me a question — they're thinking out loud and want me to engage critically with the architecture. I should validate what's strong, flag risks/tradeoffs, and maybe suggest concrete architectural choices. I should not over-explain basics — this person clearly builds tools and thinks in systems.
./workshop/parley/2026-04-03.07-16-59.100_what-graphviz-dot-language.md:279:A minimal approach that works well:
./workshop/parley/2026-04-03.07-16-59.100_what-graphviz-dot-language.md:425:💬: Yep, the real value is to have a tool that we own, and can tweak to whatever abstraction and workflow we need, with minimal learning curve, so to speak. and yes, having the same vocabulary is one core reason to have such a thing to begin with.

I think the key first step is to have structure up, and a reasonable export, to a spreadsheet, so that TPM can use to organize things.

./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:118:💬: I use nvim, is there an --editor nvim or sth?
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:122:🧠: The user wants to know if OpenShell supports neovim as an editor option. From what I covered previously, I mentioned `--editor vscode` and `--editor cursor`. The user uses nvim and wants to know if there's a similar flag. I should check the docs/source to see what editor options are supported. Let me search for this.Let me check the CLI source to see what `--editor` values are actually supported.Let me look at the actual source code to check what editor values are accepted.🧠: The user uses nvim and wants to know if OpenShell has `--editor nvim` support. From my research, the `--editor` flag only supports `vscode` and `cursor` — it generates SSH config and invokes the editor's Remote-SSH mechanism. Nvim doesn't have an equivalent built-in "remote SSH" extension that works the same way. But since OpenShell exposes SSH access to sandboxes, the user can absolutely use nvim over SSH. They just need to use `openshell sandbox ssh-config` to get the SSH config, then SSH in and run nvim inside the sandbox, or use SSHFS/remote-sshfs.nvim to mount the sandbox filesystem locally. I should be direct: no `--editor nvim`, but here are the practical paths.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:124:Short answer: **No**, `--editor` currently only supports `vscode` and `cursor`. But you don't really need it — the `--editor` flag is just a convenience wrapper that generates SSH config and launches the editor's Remote-SSH integration. For nvim, you have better options anyway.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:130:That's it. It's a thin shim. For nvim, you can do the same thing manually in ~10 seconds.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:134:**Option 1: SSH in and run nvim inside the sandbox** (simplest)
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:145:# Inside the sandbox, run nvim
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:146:nvim .
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:149:The sandbox base image is a full Linux environment. You can install nvim inside it, or better — build a custom sandbox image (`--from ./my-sandbox-dir`) with your nvim config baked in.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:151:**Option 2: SSHFS mount + local nvim** (edit locally, files live in sandbox)
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:160:# Now use local nvim
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:161:nvim ~/mnt/sandbox/
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:164:**Option 3: [remote-sshfs.nvim](https://github.com/nosduco/remote-sshfs.nvim)** (nvim-native)
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:166:remote-sshfs.nvim allows you to edit files on remote hosts via SSHFS as if they were local, loosely based on the VSCode Remote-SSH extension. Point it at the OpenShell SSH host and you get telescope integration, live grep, etc — all running over SSH to the sandbox.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:168:**Option 4: tmux inside sandbox** (most ergonomic for nvim users)
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:174:nvim .
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:177:This is probably the most natural fit. You get session persistence, and if you disconnect, your nvim session survives inside tmux in the sandbox.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:179:### Custom sandbox image with your nvim config
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:188:# Copy your nvim config
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:189:COPY nvim/ /home/sandbox/.config/nvim/
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:197:This way every sandbox you spin up has your nvim config ready to go.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:201:The `--editor` flag is a VS Code / Cursor convenience. As an nvim user, you're actually in a *better* position — nvim-over-SSH is a first-class workflow, not a bolted-on extension. The SSH config that OpenShell generates is all you need.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:203:📝: You asked whether OpenShell has `--editor nvim` support, I answered that it only supports vscode/cursor but that's just a thin SSH config wrapper — nvim users can SSH directly into the sandbox, use SSHFS, remote-sshfs.nvim, or tmux inside the sandbox, and can bake nvim config into a custom sandbox image.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:213:🧠: The user wants to create a reproducible, hermetic development environment using OpenShell custom sandbox images. They want to check in the sandbox configuration (Dockerfile, policy.yaml, etc.) into their code repo so that cloning + one command gets everything running. They're asking if this makes sense and want practical guidance. They're a nvim user on Mac, experienced with agentic coding. I should confirm this is the right approach, discuss the Dockerfile aspect, and mention that dotfiles for Mac vs Linux isn't as big a deal as they think (most nvim config, tmux config, etc. is cross-platform). I should also sketch out the repo structure and the "one command" bootstrap script.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:221:- **nvim config** — pure Lua/vimscript, works identically on Linux
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:226:The only Mac-specific bits are usually GUI tools (Hammerspoon, Raycast, etc.) and `brew`-isms — none of which matter inside a sandbox. You likely just need to copy your `~/.config/nvim/` and `~/.tmux.conf` into the image and it works.
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:236:│       ├── nvim/          # symlink or copy of your ~/.config/nvim
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:264:COPY dotfiles/nvim/ /home/sandbox/.config/nvim/
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:326:### One subtlety: nvim plugin installation
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:328:Your nvim config probably uses lazy.nvim or similar, which pulls plugins on first launch. Two options:
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:330:1. **Lazy approach** — just let it happen on first `nvim` invocation inside the sandbox. Requires github.com in your network allow list (which you'd want anyway).
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:331:2. **Hermetic approach** — add a `RUN` step in the Dockerfile that runs nvim headless to install plugins at image build time:
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:334:# After copying nvim config
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:335:RUN su sandbox -c 'nvim --headless "+Lazy! sync" +qa 2>/dev/null || true'
./workshop/parley/2026-03-28.18-37-34.270_openshell-integration.md:346:📝: You asked about making OpenShell sandbox config hermetic and repo-checked-in with one-command bootstrap, I answered with concrete repo structure (.openshell/ dir with Dockerfile, policy.yaml, dotfiles), bootstrap.sh script, noted that most dotfiles (nvim, tmux) are cross-platform so Mac→Linux is trivial, and covered nvim plugin installation strategies (lazy vs hermetic).

./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:580:- **Vim ex-commands that take an implicit current-buffer arg (`:undojoin`, `:write`, `:edit`, etc.) silently target the wrong buffer when called from async/scheduled callbacks.** `helpers.undojoin(buf)` accepted a buf param but called `vim.cmd.undojoin` directly — `:undojoin` operates on the current buffer, ignoring the param. The streaming path looked like it worked because users stay focused on the chat buffer during streaming; the longer-cadence spinner timer was more likely to fire during transient focus changes (autocmds, window switches), and its joins silently went to the wrong buffer. Fix: wrap in `vim.api.nvim_buf_call(buf, function() vim.cmd.undojoin() end)`. Rule: any helper that takes a `buf` parameter and dispatches a Vim ex-command must use `nvim_buf_call` — passing the param to the helper without enforcing buffer context is a contract the helper isn't actually upholding. Spotted in #80 second-pass debugging.
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:581:- **Sanitized snapshot in `M.get_agent` (init.lua:3570) is an allow-list, not a passthrough.** Every new field added to the agent config schema must also be appended to this snapshot, or it is silently dropped before `agent_info.resolve` ever sees it. This bit #81 (tools/max_tool_iterations/tool_result_max_bytes) and bit #118 again (synthetic_system_prompt/synthetic_system_prompt_ack) — same vector. Rule: when adding a new agent-config field, grep for `M.get_agent = function` and add it there too; ship a regression test that walks `agent record → get_agent → get_agent_info → final usage` (see `tests/unit/config_tools_spec.lua` "get_agent forwards synthetic_system_prompt config" for the pattern).
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:588:- **In autocmd callbacks, use `nvim_buf_get_name(buf)` not `ev.file`.** `ev.file` can be a relative path when the user opened the file with a relative path (e.g. `nvim workshop/file.md`). `nvim_buf_get_name(buf)` always returns the absolute path. This caused `not_chat()` to fail silently because `find_chat_root` couldn't match the relative path against configured roots.
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:589:- **After `nvim_buf_set_name` + rename, do `write!` then `edit!`.** `nvim_buf_set_name` marks the buffer as a "new file" at the new path. Without `edit!` to reload, the next manual `:w` warns "file already exists". The `write!` forces the initial write, and `edit!` clears the new-file flag.
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:617:- Fast-event callbacks: no direct `nvim_*` APIs — use `vim.schedule`
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:643:- After `edit`, use `nvim_get_current_buf()` not stale buffer variable
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:680:- **Run the FULL `make test` (lint + unit + integration) before claiming "suite green" in `--verified` — running specs individually skips the lint gate.** This session I ran each `PlenaryBustedFile` spec directly (all green) and wrote "go test/full suite green" in the close evidence — but never ran `make test`, whose FIRST target is `lint` (luacheck). A new `while pos <= #line do` (every branch returns → luacheck 542 "loop executed at most once") failed luacheck, so `make test` was RED at the gate while my Log claimed green. The boundary review caught it (FIX-THEN-SHIP), but nothing *prevented* the premature claim. Rule: the evidence for "green" is a full `make test` exit 0, not a hand-picked set of specs; individual `PlenaryBustedFile` runs are for the red→green TDD loop, not the final gate. (Bonus: luacheck flags a `while` whose body always returns — use `if` and let the iterator's repeated closure-calls do the looping.)
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:685:- **Raw `nvim_buf_set_text` is arch-forbidden (empty allow-list in `tests/arch/buffer_mutation_spec.lua`); `nvim_buf_set_lines` is allowed in `init.lua`.** #90's buffer-mutation boundary confines span edits to `buffer_edit.lua` (set_text allow-list is `{}` = zero uses). My first R1 cut used `nvim_buf_set_text` for the `[term]` wrap → `make test` red on the arch spec (lint was clean, so I only caught it at the full-suite run, not the unit loop). Fix: wrap via `nvim_buf_set_lines` (rewrite the affected whole line(s)) — the same primitive `drill_in_visual` already uses to wrap a selection, and it's on the set_lines allow-list for `init.lua`. Rule: before reaching for `nvim_buf_set_text`/`set_lines` in a non-`buffer_edit` file, check `tests/arch/buffer_mutation_spec.lua` — prefer the set_lines whole-line rewrite that existing wrappers use.
./workshop/history/000164-issue-tracker-archive-mode-sorts-by-modified-time-close-review.md:686:- **To make a decoration-only action undoable, anchor it to a text edit + reuse `projection` — `u` reverts text, never extmarks/diagnostics.** The operator wanted the define highlight+diagnostic undoable; native `u` can't touch pure decorations (review's are undoable only because a round edits text). Resolution: a minimal `[term]` bracket (one `set_lines` = one undo entry) as the anchor + reuse `skills/review/projection.lua` (`record_empty_for(pre)` + `record(post)` + `ensure_watch`). Caveat that shaped the design: `skill_render.snapshot`/`apply_snapshot` are **line-granular**, so the highlight must be whole-line `DiffChange` to round-trip; a column-precise span would not survive undo/redo. In headless tests, `:undo` does NOT fire `TextChanged` — `nvim_exec_autocmds("TextChanged", {buffer=buf})` to drive the projection watcher deterministically.

./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-m3-review.md:1:# Boundary Review — parley.nvim#116 (milestone M3)
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-m3-review.md:6:| repo | parley.nvim |
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-m3-review.md:18:Confirmed the regression. sdlc's `--issues-dir` defaults to the **relative** `"workshop/issues"`, and `dest := filepath.Join(f.IssuesDir, …)` + `os.WriteFile(dest)` writes **relative to sdlc's process cwd** (which `vim.fn.system` inherits from nvim's `getcwd()`). The old `create_issue` anchored creation at the **git root** (`get_issues_dir()` = `git_root .. "/" .. issues_dir`). So when nvim's cwd isn't the repo root, the new path diverges. I have everything I need.
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-m3-review.md:22:The M3 delegation is cleanly executed and the boundary is non-blocking: the pure/IO split is exemplary (a pure `parse_issue_new_output` + a thin `run_sdlc_issue_new` over an injectable runner), the documented sdlc stdout/stderr stream split is accurate (verified against `cmd/sdlc/issue.go`), the parser's "match the bare `.md` token, not a stream position" design is genuinely robust (I confirmed `vim.fn.system` merges stderr in this runtime, so the parser *must* skip the spaced "Created …"/warning lines — and it does), `create_issue` was removed with no orphaned callers, full suite is green (`make test` exit 0, lint 0/0/237, issues_spec OK). The one thing to fix before `sdlc close`: `cmd_issue_new` now anchors issue creation at nvim's **cwd** instead of the **git root**, a silent regression of the #142 location contract when cwd ≠ repo root.
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-m3-review.md:37:**I1 — Issue creation now anchors at nvim's cwd, not the git root (regresses the #142 location contract). `issues.lua:674`, `:361` (ARCH-DRY / ARCH-PURPOSE)**
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-m3-review.md:39:`run_sdlc_issue_new` builds `{"sdlc","issue","new",title}` with no `--issues-dir`, so sdlc uses its relative default `"workshop/issues"` and writes `filepath.Join("workshop/issues", …)` **relative to its process cwd** = nvim's `getcwd()` (`cmd/sdlc/issue.go:217,265,288`). The deleted `create_issue` resolved via `get_issues_dir()` = `git_root .. "/" .. issues_dir` (`issues.lua:391-410`), i.e. git-root-anchored and cwd-independent.
./workshop/history/000116-datatype-aware-navigation-and-creation-via-descriptor-m3-review.md:41:Failure scenario: nvim launched (or `autochdir`'d) in a subdir like `lua/parley/`. `:ParleyIssueNew` → sdlc creates a stray `lua/parley/workshop/issues/000001-foo.md` (and `NextID` scans that empty dir → **allocates a colliding ID**, e.g. `000001`), then parley's `fnamemodify(path, ":p")` (relative to the same cwd) opens that stray file. The #142 prompt label still shows the *git-root* repo basename via `get_issues_repo_root()` — so the label now lies about where the file lands. Common case (cwd = repo root) is unaffected; no crash or data loss, hence Important not Critical, but it silently misfiles + can collide IDs.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:1:# Boundary Review — parley.nvim#161 (whole-issue close)
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:6:| repo | parley.nvim |
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:18:I have enough to finalize. I traced the DRY refactor line-by-line, verified every Core-concepts row against the code, confirmed the visibility mechanism (`diag_display.set(true)` at setup, `init.lua:776`), and independently ran the specs: define unit (10), define integration (11), drill_in (108), keybindings (20), chat_respond (29) — all green — plus luacheck clean on all 7 changed files.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:29:- **ARCH-DRY refactor is faithful, not just plausible.** `drill_in_visual` now calls `define.slice_selection(lines_in_range, 1, sc-1, er-sr+1, ec-1)` (`init.lua:1557`). I traced single-line and multi-line branches against the original inline logic (including the retained `ec` clamp at `init.lua:1549`) — behavior is identical, and `tests/unit/drill_in_spec.lua` (108 cases) passes. One slice implementation shared by two callers.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:32:- **`skill_invoke` seams are minimal and backward-safe.** `opts.no_reload` gates both the pre-query `silent write` (`skill_invoke.lua:135`) and the on-exit reload (`:234`); `opts.document or original` (`:156`) defaults to prior behavior when absent. The no-write-on-dirty-buffer test is a real behavioral assertion (file bytes unchanged + `&modified` still true), exactly the kind that catches a draft-persistence regression.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:43:- **The keybinding-split test hand-mirrors the callback table instead of exercising the real `prep_chat` wiring** (`tests/integration/define_spec.lua:279–329`). It builds a local `callbacks = { chat_define = { v=define, … } }` and feeds it to `register_buffer`, so it verifies the config split (real: `<M-CR>` only in `chat_shortcut_define`, `<C-g><C-g>` only in `chat_shortcut_respond`) and `register_buffer`'s per-mode dispatch — but **not** that the production IIFE at `init.lua:2054` actually returns `{ n=respond, i=respond, v=define_v, x=define_v }` keyed under id `"chat_define"`. The plan's own implementer note warns a key/id mismatch "silently no-ops the binding"; that exact failure mode is invisible to this test. Wiring is in fact correct (I verified id/config_key/`callbacks[entry.id]` all agree), but the regression guard is weaker than it reads. *Fix (cheap):* assert `nvim_buf_get_keymap` after a real `prep_chat`, or at minimum pull the real callback table out of the prep path rather than re-declaring it.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:47:- `make_respond_cb("ChatRespond")` is constructed twice — once for `chat_respond` (`init.lua:2052`) and again inside the `chat_define` IIFE (`:2055`). Reuse one instance for both to avoid the parallel closure set.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:48:- `render_definition` anchors the diagnostic only to the selection's first line (`end_lnum = sel_line0`, `init.lua:1619`) even for multi-line selections. Consistent with the documented "line-granular anchor" v1 limitation, not a bug.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:49:- The plan's Task 10 Step 4 manual-acceptance steps (visible pop-under, web-cited obscure term, draft-not-written) aren't recorded in `## Log`. The visibility mechanism is reliable — `diag_display.set(true)` runs at `setup()` (`init.lua:776`), applying `virtual_lines{current_line=true}` to the `parley_skill` namespace session-wide — but the record is missing.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:59:- Two `find_exchange_at_line` implementations coexist (`chat_parser.lua:144` returns `idx`; `init.lua:3156` returns `idx, component`). `context_for_selection` uses only the first value so it's safe with either, but this pre-existing duplication is a latent DRY item for a future consolidation — not introduced here.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:72:| repo | parley.nvim |
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:97:- **ARCH-DRY refactor is faithful, not just plausible.** `drill_in_visual` now calls `define.slice_selection(lines_in_range, 1, sc-1, er-sr+1, ec-1)` (`init.lua:1557`). I traced single- and multi-line branches against the original inline logic (including the retained `ec` clamp at `init.lua:1549`, which makes `math.min(c2+1,#line)` a no-op there) — behavior is identical, and `tests/unit/drill_in_spec.lua` (108) passes. One slice implementation, two callers.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:100:- **`skill_invoke` seams are minimal and backward-safe.** `opts.no_reload` gates both the pre-query `silent write` (`skill_invoke.lua:135`) and the on-exit reload (`:234`); `opts.document or original` (`:156`) defaults to prior behavior when absent. The no-write-on-dirty-buffer test asserts real bytes (file unchanged + `&modified` still true) — exactly the regression it must catch.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:101:- **R1 undo/redo reuses review's projection correctly.** `record_empty_for(original)` + `record(bracketed)` + `ensure_watch`, bracketed by `set_applying` (`init.lua:1634,1659-1662`); the whole-line `DiffChange` (`skill_render.highlight_line`, `:160`) round-trips through the line-granular snapshot. Integration test drives `u`/`<C-r>` and asserts both the bracket text and the decoration state.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:109:- **No regression guard for the bounded-context path against the real parser** (`define.lua:49-66`; test gap). `context_for_selection` reads `ex.question.line_start` / `ex.answer.line_end` / `ex.question.line_end`. The *unit* test (`tests/unit/define_spec.lua`) exercises the exchange-found path only with a **synthetic** `parsed_chat` + injected `finder`, and the one integration test that reaches the real `parse_chat` (`define_visual + render_definition`) uses a buffer with **no chat exchanges** (`{"line one","line two","here is ASIN in context",…}`, `define_spec.lua`), so it hits the whole-buffer *fallback* — never the sliced-exchange branch. The `sends opts.document as the user message` test bypasses `define_visual` entirely, passing `document = "SENTINEL CONTEXT"` straight to `invoke`. Net: the interaction between the live parser's output shape and `context_for_selection`'s field access has zero coverage. I verified the fields currently match (`init.lua:3195`, `chat_parser.lua:144`, `parse_chat` exchange shape), so this is a *latent* gap, not a live bug — but a future rename in `parse_chat` would silently degrade define to whole-buffer context (defeating Spec Component 1's design decision) with no failing test. *Fix (cheap):* one integration case — a chat buffer with a real exchange, select a term inside it, assert the payload user-message contains only the enclosing exchange's lines (not the whole buffer).
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:113:- `lua/parley/tools/init.lua` was modified (added `emit_definition` to `BUILTIN_NAMES`, `:167`) but isn't listed under `chat/inline_define.code` in `atlas/traceability.yaml` — the traceability code list is slightly incomplete.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:114:- `apply_snapshot` hardcodes `source = "parley-skill"` (`skill_render.lua:200`), so after a redo the define diagnostic's `source` changes from `"parley-define"` (`init.lua:1653`) to `"parley-skill"`. Cosmetic only.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:115:- If the model runs `web_search` but omits `emit_definition`, `render_definition`'s `if not call then return end` (`init.lua:1614`) no-ops with no user feedback (unlike the buffer-changed path, which warns). A one-line "no definition returned" notice would close the silent-nothing-happened UX gap. Non-blocking.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:116:- `render_definition` anchors the diagnostic to the selection's first line only (`end_lnum = e.first0`, `init.lua:1649`) even for multi-line selections — consistent with the documented "line-granular anchor" v1 limitation, not a bug.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:126:- Two `find_exchange_at_line` implementations coexist (`chat_parser.lua:144` → `idx`; `init.lua:3195` → `idx, component`). `context_for_selection` reads only the first return, so it's safe with either, but this pre-existing duplication is a latent DRY item for a future consolidation — not introduced here.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:140:| repo | parley.nvim |
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:163:- **ARCH-DRY refactor is byte-faithful, verified independently.** I traced `drill_in_visual`'s new call `define.slice_selection(lines_in_range, 1, sc-1, er-sr+1, ec-1)` (`init.lua:1557`) against the deleted inline logic for both single- and multi-line branches, including the retained `ec` clamp (`init.lua:1549`). Lua's `sub` auto-clamps, so `math.min(c2+1,#line)` is behavior-preserving. One slice implementation, two callers; `drill_in_spec` (108) stays green in the full run.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:166:- **skill_invoke seams are minimal, backward-safe, and single-turn.** `opts.no_reload` gates both the pre-query `silent write` (`skill_invoke.lua:135`) and the on-exit reload (`:234`); `opts.document or original` (`:156`) defaults to prior behavior. Confirmed the flow is single-turn (no agentic loop), so the no-op `emit_definition` handler causes no extra round-trip, and the `_gen[buf] ~= gen` guard (`:199`) prevents a superseded re-invoke from double-bracketing.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:167:- **R1 undo/redo reuse of review's projection is sound.** `set_applying`-bracketed edit → `record_empty_for(original)` + `record(bracketed)` + `ensure_watch` (`init.lua:1639-1667`); the whole-line `DiffChange` (`skill_render.highlight_line`, `:160`) round-trips through the line-granular snapshot (`snapshot`/`apply_snapshot` capture both the `parley_skill_hl` extmarks and `parley_skill` diagnostics). `define_spec` drives `u`/`<C-r>` and asserts both bracket text and decoration state.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:181:- `apply_snapshot` hardcodes `source = "parley-skill"` (`skill_render.lua:200`), so after a redo the define diagnostic's `source` flips from `"parley-define"` (`init.lua:1658`) to `"parley-skill"`. Cosmetic.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:182:- `render_definition` anchors the diagnostic to the selection's first line only (`end_lnum = e.first0`, `init.lua:1654`) even for multi-line selections — consistent with the documented "line-granular anchor" v1 limitation, not a bug.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:187:- Strong on the risk surface: pure core (no IO), no-reload/no-write (asserts real file bytes + `&modified`), document-override, no-tool-call no-op, whitespace no-op, registration, web-toggle payload (hits real `prepare_payload`), the real `prep_chat`/`maparg` wiring, the real-`parse_chat` field contract, and the R1 bracket/highlight/undo/redo round-trip. I independently ran the full `make test`: lint OK on all changed files (`define.lua`, `init.lua` both OK); define unit + integration, `drill_in`, `buffer_mutation` (arch), and `review_projection` specs all PASS.
./workshop/history/000161-inline-term-definition-close-review.md:194:- Two `find_exchange_at_line` implementations coexist (`chat_parser.lua:144` → `idx`; `init.lua:3200` → `idx, component`). `context_for_selection` reads only the first return, so it's safe with either — pre-existing latent DRY item, not introduced here.

./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:21:    init.lua       -- mechanical definition (args, completion, hooks)
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:24:    init.lua
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:30:`init.lua` returns a table:
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:89:3. **Read buffer content** — `vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines`.
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:151:Skills are discovered lazily on first `<C-g>s` press or first programmatic `skill_runner.run()` call (not at setup time). This avoids scanning the filesystem during startup. `skill_runner` scans `lua/parley/skills/*/init.lua`, `require`'s each, and caches the returned tables. Subsequent calls use the cache.
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:184:**init.lua**:
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:190:Marker parsing functions (`parse_markers`, `parse_marker_sections`, `populate_quickfix`) live in `skills/review/init.lua`.
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:192:The `<C-g>vi` keybinding (insert ㊷ marker) remains as a buffer-local keymap on markdown files — it's an editing aid, not a skill invocation. Stays in review's init.lua, wired via `setup_keymaps` called from init.lua's `setup_markdown_keymaps`.
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:202:**init.lua**:
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:213:lua/parley/skills/review/init.lua        -- review skill definition + marker logic
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:215:lua/parley/skills/voice_apply/init.lua   -- voice-apply skill definition
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:230:| `parse_markers` | review.lua | skills/review/init.lua |
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:231:| `parse_marker_sections` | review.lua | skills/review/init.lua |
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:232:| `populate_quickfix` | review.lua | skills/review/init.lua |
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:233:| `setup_keymaps` (㊷ insert) | review.lua | skills/review/init.lua |
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:236:Thin shim that requires `skills/review/init.lua` and exposes `setup_keymaps` for init.lua's `setup_markdown_keymaps` call. May be eliminated entirely if init.lua can wire directly to the skill module.
./workshop/history/000106-skill-system-plan.md:263:**Architecture:** Extract shared edit-apply-display code from review.lua into skill_runner.lua. Skills are folders under lua/parley/skills/ with init.lua (mechanics) and SKILL.md (prompt). A picker (skill_picker.lua) provides the `<C-g>s` entry point with cascading typeahead.

./workshop/history/000116-discovery-registry-plan.md:9:**Tech Stack:** Lua (Neovim plugin), `plenary.nvim` headless test harness (`make test`), `rg` for content/glob discovery. Follows parley module conventions (`local M = {}` … `return M`) and the existing `lua/parley/tools/` registry pattern.
./workshop/history/000116-discovery-registry-plan.md:65:| `RegistryBuilder` | `lua/parley/discovery/init.lua` | new | repo-mode state + base ∪ local |
./workshop/history/000116-discovery-registry-plan.md:67:- **LocalTypeDiscovery** — discovers a repo's *novel* `type:` values: `rg -o '^type: \w+'` over the repo root, `sort -u`, minus the base names. Each surviving value becomes a minimal `local` TypeDescriptor (`matcher = frontmatter type=value`, `locate = {"**/*.md"}`, `blurb` synthesized). This is the cheap, grep-backed *production* behind the registry interface.

./workshop/history/000116-discovery-registry-plan.md:191:- [x] **Step 3: Implement** `M.build(ctx)` → `Registry.of(base ∪ deduped local)`. Reuse `super_repo.compute_members` for the member list _(transitively, via `config.super_repo_members` which `super_repo.activate` populates — no recompute)_. Wire `parley.discovery.build`/`current()` in `init.lua`. **Multi-root (the merge):** repo-relative `locate` globs expanded across `[repo_root] + members`; absolute/global globs pass through unchanged. _members already include the current repo, so they supersede the lone repo_root._

./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:36:  - **Future extensions:** a minimal nested+list YAML emitter fallback **iff** the gating task proves cliproxy rejects JSON (spec §Emission).
./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:48:| `Cliproxy` commands | `lua/parley/cliproxy.lua` + `init.lua` | new | user commands |
./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:66:- **Cliproxy.spawn(binary, config_path)** — `vim.uv.spawn` **detached** (`detached=true`, `uv.unref` the handle) so the daemon outlives nvim and is shared; records our PID so `stop` only kills a parley-spawned proxy.
./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:70:- **Cliproxy commands** — `:ParleyProxy status|start|stop|restart|login`, registered in `init.lua` under `M.config.cmd_prefix`. `login <provider>` shells out to `cliproxyapi -<provider>-login [-no-browser]` (per-provider flags — `claude`, `codex`, `codex-device`, `google`→`-login`, `kimi`, `xai`, `antigravity`; NOT a `login` subcommand — confirmed in Task 2.0); `status` reports binary source, health/auth state (incl. `needs_login`), host:port, auth-dir, rendered-config path, and config-drift.
./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:83:- **fake_cliproxy** — a real subprocess (Lua via `nvim -l`, or a tiny TCP listener) used by integration tests. Modes: `healthy` (answers identity route 200), `foreign` (200 wrong shape), `unauth` (401), `slow` (never healthy), `crash` (exits immediately). Process-level per AGENTS.md external-service rule — function mocks would miss the reuse-vs-spawn and identity-classification bugs.
./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:158:- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**
./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:365:- [ ] **Step 1: Build the fake** — `tests/fixtures/fake_cliproxy.lua`: a `vim.uv` TCP server (run via `nvim -l`) answering **`GET /v1/models`** per a `--mode` arg (and `--port`):
./workshop/history/000131-managed-cliproxy-plan.md:399:  1. read the merged config via `require("parley").config.cliproxy` (NOT the module's own `M` — `cliproxy.lua`'s `M` is the module table; the merged user config lives on the `parley` module, set in init.lua:386-388). Same for `is_managed()`. If `manage` is not true → `callback()` immediately (no-op path).

./workshop/history/000160-navigate-refs-plan.md:9:**Tech Stack:** Lua / Neovim (plugin), `vim.system` for the shell-out, `vim.json` for `--json`, plenary.nvim (busted-style) tests. Depends on ariadne#144's `sdlc resolve` (merged to ariadne main).
./workshop/history/000160-navigate-refs-plan.md:53:| `M.cmd.ResolveRefUnderCursor` | `lua/parley/init.lua` | new | cursor/buffer + open/picker |
./workshop/history/000160-navigate-refs-plan.md:55:| `resolve_ref` registry entry + wiring | `lua/parley/keybinding_registry.lua` + `init.lua` | modified | keymap |
./workshop/history/000160-navigate-refs-plan.md:60:- **ResolveRefUnderCursor** — the keymap handler: read `line`/`col` (`nvim_win_get_cursor`), `parse_ref_at_cursor` → ref (or notify "no ref under cursor"), compute `cwd` via `neighborhood.for_buf(0)`, `run_resolve(ref, {cwd=cwd}, on_done)`. `on_done`: 0 files → notify sdlc's error; 1 → `open_buf(path, true)`; N → `float_picker.open` of the family. Modeled on `issues.cmd_issue_goto` (`issues.lua:894`) + `issue_finder.lua:227`.
./workshop/history/000160-navigate-refs-plan.md:64:- **resolve_ref registry entry + wiring** — a `keybinding_registry.M.entries` row `{ id="resolve_ref", config_key="chat_shortcut_resolve_ref", default_key="gf", default_modes={"n"}, scope="parley_buffer", buffer_local=true, desc=… }` (template: `open_file`, `:416`); callback `resolve_ref = M.cmd.ResolveRefUnderCursor` added to the `callbacks` at both `register_buffer` sites (`init.lua:1972` chat, `init.lua:2219` markdown). Help text auto-generates from the registry.

./workshop/history/000133-review-modes-journal-plan.md:331:- [ ] **Step 3: Implement** — remove the submission-time pending-`{}` abort (the `if #pending > 0 then populate_quickfix; return` block at `init.lua:485-493`). The agent already skips non-ready markers (SKILL.md contract), so submission processes ready markers and leaves `{}` ones. Leave `scan_on_enter` / `BufWritePost` quickfix untouched — pending markers still surface **on save**. **Unified terminal logic for the `on_done` resubmit loop (`init.lua:529-542`)** — replace the current `has_questions` early-stop branch entirely. New rule, stated explicitly so the loop isn't left subtly wrong:
./workshop/history/000133-review-modes-journal-plan.md:408:- Modify: `lua/parley/skills/review/init.lua` (`run_via_invoke` `on_done`)
./workshop/history/000133-review-modes-journal-plan.md:444:- Modify: `lua/parley/keybinding_registry.lua` (add `review_menu` + `review_next` entries), `lua/parley/config.lua` (shortcut defaults), `lua/parley/skills/review/init.lua` (`setup_keymaps`)
./workshop/history/000133-review-modes-journal-plan.md:447:- [ ] **Step 1: Write failing test** — opening a markdown review doc sets buffer-local `<M-o>` (open menu) and `<M-CR>` (open menu pre-selected to sticky mode, then run). Assert via `nvim_buf_get_keymap`.
./workshop/history/000133-review-modes-journal-plan.md:449:- [ ] **Step 3: Implement** — add `config.review_shortcut_menu = { modes = {"n"}, shortcut = "<M-o>" }` and `config.review_shortcut_next = { modes = {"n","i"}, shortcut = "<M-CR>" }`. **Follow the existing `review_edit` pattern** (`keybinding_registry.lua:691` is `help_only=true`, registered "by review skill"; the actual `vim.keymap.set` lives in `setup_keymaps`, `init.lua:2089`): add `help_only=true` registry entries for help output, and do the real buffer-local binding in `setup_keymaps` — do NOT route through the generic `register_buffer` path. Bind `<M-o>` → `review_menu.open(buf, { on_submit = run })` and `<M-CR>` → open menu pre-selected to sticky mode (same callback). Both call `review.run_via_invoke(buf, { mode, instruction })`. `<M-CR>` "always works" — opens the menu even on a doc not yet in review (no session gate). Verified free: `<M-CR>` chat-respond is `chat`-scope (markdown buffers register only `{parley_buffer, markdown}`), and `<M-o>` is unbound today.
./workshop/history/000133-review-modes-journal-plan.md:455:- [ ] **Step 1:** In a real nvim on a scratch markdown doc with no markers: `<M-o>` → pick **developmental** + type "expand these bullets into prose" → confirm whole-doc edits land, highlighted, with gutter explanations; confirm a `<doc>.parley-journal.md` sidecar appears with round 1 (mode, diff, rationale).

./workshop/history/000120-update-to-branching-mode.md:64:- `chat_toggle_raw_request` (`<C-g>r`): registry entry deleted, callback wiring in `init.lua` removed.
./workshop/history/000120-update-to-branching-mode.md:93:- [x] `<C-g>q` (visual mode only) wired in registry + `prep_chat`. Selection wraps as `🤖{T}[]`, cursor inside `[]`, `startinsert`. Multi-line selection works; uses `nvim_buf_set_lines` with reconstructed prefix/suffix to stay within the buffer-mutation arch policy.
./workshop/history/000120-update-to-branching-mode.md:121:- Buffer-mutation arch test (#90) caught two of my early calls (`nvim_buf_set_text` in init.lua's drill-in handler, and `nvim_buf_set_lines` in chat_respond.lua). Resolved by (a) rewriting the visual handler to compute prefix/suffix and use `set_lines` from init.lua (already allow-listed), (b) adding `buffer_edit.replace_all_lines` and routing chat_respond's full-buffer rewrite through it.

./workshop/history/000086-hard-to-spot-which-test-failed-in-make-test.md:16:`test-unit` and `test-integration` ran `xargs -P 8 -I {} nvim ... PlenaryBustedFile {}`. With 8 parallel jobs the per-file plenary output interleaved line-by-line, and on failure the only summary was `Unit tests failed` — the failing file path was technically present in plenary's chatter but invisible amid 50+ files of pass output. xargs also doesn't natively label which input produced which output, so even reading the noise didn't tell you which spec failed.
./workshop/history/000086-hard-to-spot-which-test-failed-in-make-test.md:27:- [x] Wrap the per-file `nvim … PlenaryBustedFile` invocation in a small shell wrapper that captures stdout/stderr to a temp file, prints `PASS: <path>` on success or `===FAIL: <path>===` followed by the indented captured output on failure, and appends failing paths to a shared `$FAILED_LOG`.
./workshop/history/000086-hard-to-spot-which-test-failed-in-make-test.md:40:- Diagnosed: `Makefile.parley:41-54` ran `xargs -P 8 -I {} nvim … PlenaryBustedFile {} || echo "Unit tests failed"`. xargs doesn't label outputs and the parallel streams interleave; failure path is buried.

./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:803:- **Vim ex-commands that take an implicit current-buffer arg (`:undojoin`, `:write`, `:edit`, etc.) silently target the wrong buffer when called from async/scheduled callbacks.** `helpers.undojoin(buf)` accepted a buf param but called `vim.cmd.undojoin` directly — `:undojoin` operates on the current buffer, ignoring the param. The streaming path looked like it worked because users stay focused on the chat buffer during streaming; the longer-cadence spinner timer was more likely to fire during transient focus changes (autocmds, window switches), and its joins silently went to the wrong buffer. Fix: wrap in `vim.api.nvim_buf_call(buf, function() vim.cmd.undojoin() end)`. Rule: any helper that takes a `buf` parameter and dispatches a Vim ex-command must use `nvim_buf_call` — passing the param to the helper without enforcing buffer context is a contract the helper isn't actually upholding. Spotted in #80 second-pass debugging.
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:804:- **Sanitized snapshot in `M.get_agent` (init.lua:3570) is an allow-list, not a passthrough.** Every new field added to the agent config schema must also be appended to this snapshot, or it is silently dropped before `agent_info.resolve` ever sees it. This bit #81 (tools/max_tool_iterations/tool_result_max_bytes) and bit #118 again (synthetic_system_prompt/synthetic_system_prompt_ack) — same vector. Rule: when adding a new agent-config field, grep for `M.get_agent = function` and add it there too; ship a regression test that walks `agent record → get_agent → get_agent_info → final usage` (see `tests/unit/config_tools_spec.lua` "get_agent forwards synthetic_system_prompt config" for the pattern).
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:811:- **In autocmd callbacks, use `nvim_buf_get_name(buf)` not `ev.file`.** `ev.file` can be a relative path when the user opened the file with a relative path (e.g. `nvim workshop/file.md`). `nvim_buf_get_name(buf)` always returns the absolute path. This caused `not_chat()` to fail silently because `find_chat_root` couldn't match the relative path against configured roots.
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:812:- **After `nvim_buf_set_name` + rename, do `write!` then `edit!`.** `nvim_buf_set_name` marks the buffer as a "new file" at the new path. Without `edit!` to reload, the next manual `:w` warns "file already exists". The `write!` forces the initial write, and `edit!` clears the new-file flag.
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:840:- Fast-event callbacks: no direct `nvim_*` APIs — use `vim.schedule`
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:866:- After `edit`, use `nvim_get_current_buf()` not stale buffer variable
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:903:- **Run the FULL `make test` (lint + unit + integration) before claiming "suite green" in `--verified` — running specs individually skips the lint gate.** This session I ran each `PlenaryBustedFile` spec directly (all green) and wrote "go test/full suite green" in the close evidence — but never ran `make test`, whose FIRST target is `lint` (luacheck). A new `while pos <= #line do` (every branch returns → luacheck 542 "loop executed at most once") failed luacheck, so `make test` was RED at the gate while my Log claimed green. The boundary review caught it (FIX-THEN-SHIP), but nothing *prevented* the premature claim. Rule: the evidence for "green" is a full `make test` exit 0, not a hand-picked set of specs; individual `PlenaryBustedFile` runs are for the red→green TDD loop, not the final gate. (Bonus: luacheck flags a `while` whose body always returns — use `if` and let the iterator's repeated closure-calls do the looping.)
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:908:- **Raw `nvim_buf_set_text` is arch-forbidden (empty allow-list in `tests/arch/buffer_mutation_spec.lua`); `nvim_buf_set_lines` is allowed in `init.lua`.** #90's buffer-mutation boundary confines span edits to `buffer_edit.lua` (set_text allow-list is `{}` = zero uses). My first R1 cut used `nvim_buf_set_text` for the `[term]` wrap → `make test` red on the arch spec (lint was clean, so I only caught it at the full-suite run, not the unit loop). Fix: wrap via `nvim_buf_set_lines` (rewrite the affected whole line(s)) — the same primitive `drill_in_visual` already uses to wrap a selection, and it's on the set_lines allow-list for `init.lua`. Rule: before reaching for `nvim_buf_set_text`/`set_lines` in a non-`buffer_edit` file, check `tests/arch/buffer_mutation_spec.lua` — prefer the set_lines whole-line rewrite that existing wrappers use.
./workshop/history/000167-define-diagnostic-highlight-span-close-review.md:909:- **To make a decoration-only action undoable, anchor it to a text edit + reuse `projection` — `u` reverts text, never extmarks/diagnostics.** The operator wanted the define highlight+diagnostic undoable; native `u` can't touch pure decorations (review's are undoable only because a round edits text). Resolution: a minimal `[term]` bracket (one `set_lines` = one undo entry) as the anchor + reuse `skills/review/projection.lua` (`record_empty_for(pre)` + `record(post)` + `ensure_watch`). Caveat that shaped the design: `skill_render.snapshot`/`apply_snapshot` are **line-granular**, so the highlight must be whole-line `DiffChange` to round-trip; a column-precise span would not survive undo/redo. In headless tests, `:undo` does NOT fire `TextChanged` — `nvim_exec_autocmds("TextChanged", {buffer=buf})` to drive the projection watcher deterministically.

./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor-for-pure-position-model.md:59:2. **`lua/parley/render_buffer.lua` (NEW, pure)** — `render_section`, `render_exchange`, `positions`, plus standalone helpers (`agent_header_lines`, `raw_request_fence_lines`). No buffer access, no nvim API beyond `vim.json/vim.tbl_*`. Goes in `PURE_FILES` arch list.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor-for-pure-position-model.md:60:3. **`lua/parley/buffer_edit.lua` (NEW)** — single entry point for all `nvim_buf_set_lines`/`nvim_buf_set_text` calls in the plugin. Returns opaque `PosHandle` (extmark-backed) so callers chain operations without ever computing line offsets.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor-for-pure-position-model.md:61:4. **`tests/arch/arch_helper.lua` (NEW)** — architectural fitness functions. `assert_pattern_scoping({pattern, scope, allow_only_in, rationale, ignore_comments})`. Initial 3 rules: `nvim_buf_set_lines` only in `buffer_edit.lua`; `nvim_buf_set_text` only in `buffer_edit.lua`; pure files contain no `vim.api/cmd/schedule/defer_fn`.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor-for-pure-position-model.md:67:**Definition of done**: `grep -rn "nvim_buf_set_lines\|nvim_buf_set_text" lua/parley/` returns ONLY `lua/parley/buffer_edit.lua`. `chat_respond.lua` ≥300 lines lighter. All ~406 tests green (~321 existing + ~85 new). Manual verification: vanilla chat byte-identical, ClaudeAgentTools round-trip clean.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor-for-pure-position-model.md:83:    - **Chunk 3 (Phase 3)**: tool_loop and dispatcher streaming migrated. Final arch state: `nvim_buf_set_lines` only in `buffer_edit.lua` (within the chat-rendering pipeline). Commits: aae15c8, ff7f51d.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor-for-pure-position-model.md:85:  - **Picker/UI helpers deferred**: chat_finder, init, vision, issues, float_picker, config, system_prompt_picker, highlighter all still use raw `nvim_buf_set_lines`. Migrating them is desirable for consistency but YAGNI for #90's renderer scope. They are listed in the arch test allow list with the explicit "deferred follow-up" comment.
./workshop/history/000090-renderer-refactor-for-pure-position-model.md:87:  - **Chunk 4 (Phase 4 — re-validation)** is up next: full make test green ✓, manual nvim verification (vanilla chat + ClaudeAgentTools tool round-trip), live Anthropic harness against `one-round-tool-use.md`, then resume #81 M2 Task 2.7.
